title: C Pointers
description: Understand pointers in C — one of the most powerful (and misunderstood) features of the language.

sections.title: What is a Pointer?
sections.lesson:
A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Instead of holding a value directly, it holds the location where that value is stored in memory.

Two key operators:

  • & (address-of) — gets the memory address of a variable
  • * (dereference) — accesses the value at the address a pointer holds
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int x = 42;
    int *ptr = &x;   // ptr stores the address of x

    printf("%d\n",  x);    // 42   (value of x)
    printf("%p\n",  ptr);  // 0x... (address of x)
    printf("%d\n", *ptr);  // 42   (value AT the address)

    *ptr = 100;            // change x through ptr
    printf("%d\n",  x);    // 100
    return 0;
}

The pointer type must match the variable type it points to: int * for int, float * for float, etc.

questions.question: What does a pointer variable store?
questions.explanation: A pointer stores the memory address of another variable — not the value itself. Use * (dereference) to access the value at that address.

questions.question: Which operator gives you the memory address of a variable?
questions.explanation: The & (address-of) operator returns the memory address of a variable. Example: int *p = &x; makes p point to x.

questions.question: Given int x = 5; int *p = &x; what does *p return?
questions.explanation: The * (dereference) operator accesses the value stored at the address held by the pointer. *p reads the value of x, which is 5.

sections.title: Pointer Arithmetic
sections.lesson:
You can perform arithmetic on pointers. When you add 1 to a pointer, it advances by the size of the data type it points to — not just 1 byte.

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int arr[] = {10, 20, 30, 40};
    int *ptr = arr;  // ptr points to arr[0]

    printf("%d\n", *ptr);    // 10
    ptr++;                    // advance by sizeof(int)
    printf("%d\n", *ptr);    // 20
    ptr++;                    // advance again
    printf("%d\n", *ptr);    // 30

    // Can also use offset notation
    printf("%d\n", *(arr + 3)); // 40
    return 0;
}

On a typical 64-bit system, int is 4 bytes. So incrementing an int* moves it 4 bytes forward in memory, landing on the next integer.

TypeTypical Sizeptr++ moves
char *1 byte1 byte
int *4 bytes4 bytes
double *8 bytes8 bytes

questions.question: If int *ptr points to arr[0], what does ptr++ point to?
questions.explanation: Pointer arithmetic advances by the size of the pointed-to type. For int* on most systems, ptr++ moves 4 bytes forward — to the next int in the array (arr[1]).

questions.question: What does *(arr + 2) evaluate to if arr = {5, 10, 15, 20}?
questions.explanation: arr + 2 points to the element at index 2. Dereferencing it gives the value there, which is 15.

sections.title: Pointers and Arrays
sections.lesson:
In C, an array name is essentially a pointer to the first element. This is why arrays and pointers are so closely related.

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
    int *ptr = arr;  // same as: int *ptr = &arr[0]

    // These are equivalent:
    printf("%d\n", arr[1]);   // 20 - array notation
    printf("%d\n", *(arr+1)); // 20 - pointer arithmetic
    printf("%d\n", ptr[1]);   // 20 - pointer as array
    printf("%d\n", *(ptr+1)); // 20 - dereferencing
    return 0;
}

Key distinction: arr is a constant pointer — you can't do arr++. But if you copy the address to ptr, you can increment ptr freely.

// Iterating an array with a pointer
for (int *p = arr; p < arr + 3; p++) {
    printf("%d ", *p);  // 10 20 30
}

questions.question: In C, an array name is essentially a ________ to the first element.
questions.explanation: An array name decays to a pointer to its first element. arr and &arr[0] are equivalent in most contexts.

questions.question: Are arr[i] and *(arr + i) equivalent in C?
questions.explanation: arr[i] is syntactic sugar for *(arr + i). The compiler converts the bracket notation to pointer arithmetic internally.

questions.question: Why can't you do arr++ if arr is an array name?
questions.explanation: An array name is a constant pointer to the first element — its address cannot be changed. You'd need to copy it to a pointer variable (int *p = arr) first.

sections.title: Pointers and Functions
sections.lesson:
By default, C passes arguments by value — functions get a copy. To modify the original variable, pass its address (pointer).

Pass by value (no change to original):

void addOne(int n) {
    n++;  // only modifies the local copy
}

int x = 5;
addOne(x);
printf("%d", x); // still 5

Pass by pointer (modifies original):

void addOne(int *n) {
    (*n)++;  // modifies the value at the address
}

int x = 5;
addOne(&x);      // pass address of x
printf("%d", x); // now 6

Classic example — swapping two variables:

void swap(int *a, int *b) {
    int temp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = temp;
}

int x = 1, y = 2;
swap(&x, &y);
// x = 2, y = 1

questions.question: How do you allow a function to modify a variable declared outside it?
choices: Pass a pointer to the variable
questions.answer: Pass a pointer to the variable
questions.explanation: To modify an external variable inside a function, pass its address (pointer). The function then uses * to dereference and modify the original value.

questions.question: In the call swap(&x, &y), what does & do?
choices: Passes the address of x and y
Declares x and y as pointers
questions.answer: Passes the address of x and y
questions.explanation: & is the address-of operator. swap(&x, &y) passes the memory addresses of x and y so the function can modify them directly.

sections.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
sections.lesson:
Dynamic memory lets you allocate memory at runtime using the heap, rather than declaring fixed-size arrays at compile time.

Four key functions from <stdlib.h>:

FunctionPurpose
malloc(size)Allocate bytes (uninitialized)
calloc(n, size)Allocate n×size bytes (zero-initialized)
realloc(ptr, size)Resize previously allocated block
free(ptr)Release allocated memory
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    int n = 5;
    int *arr = (int *)malloc(n * sizeof(int));

    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Memory allocation failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        arr[i] = (i + 1) * 10;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);  // 10 20 30 40 50

    free(arr);  // Always free!
    arr = NULL; // Prevent dangling pointer
    return 0;
}

Always call free() when done — failing to do so causes a memory leak.

questions.question: Which function allocates memory and initializes it to zero?
questions.explanation: calloc(n, size) allocates n elements of given size and initializes all bytes to zero. malloc() allocates without initialization.

questions.question: What happens if you forget to call free() after malloc()?
questions.explanation: Forgetting to free() heap-allocated memory causes a memory leak — the memory remains reserved and unavailable for the rest of the program's lifetime.

questions.question: What should you always check after calling malloc()?
choices: If the return value is NULL
If sizeof returned the right size
questions.answer: If the return value is NULL
questions.explanation: malloc() returns NULL if allocation fails (e.g., out of memory). Always check before using the pointer to avoid undefined behavior.
